actions.thought: Holding AKs on the button is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and exert pressure. A standard opening of 3x the big blind (300 chips) is appropriate here to narrow the field or take the blinds and antes immediately.

I am in the small blind with 42o, which is a very weak starting hand. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice here, especially since I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a button raise. There is no incentive to defend such a poor hand against a raise, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

Our hand is Q3o, which is extremely weak and completely disconnected. Facing a 3x open from the Button, we have negative expected value calling or raising. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game holding Ace-Four offsuit. This is a strong enough hand to play aggressively in a short-handed format. Raising to 250 allows me to take the betting lead, put pressure on the blinds, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or control the hand if called.
K3o is a very weak starting hand, especially when facing a pre-flop raise. Playing this out of position against the button is unprofitable, so the standard and correct decision is to simply fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine of spades. Flashlight raised from the Button to 250 and Pro folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 550, giving me excellent pot odds. Ten-Nine suited is a strong hand that plays well post-flop, especially in a heads-up situation. Calling is the standard play here to defend my blind and see a flop without bloating the pot out of position.

I have middle pair (Tens) on a board of Q-T-2. This is a marginal made hand with some showdown value. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them. Leading out would bloat the pot with a hand that is likely either way ahead of their bluffs or way behind their top pairs/sets. By checking, I can evaluate Flashlight's continuation bet sizing and decide whether to call or fold, keeping the pot manageable.

The flop of Q-T-2 is relatively dry. While I haven't connected with the board, my pre-flop aggression warrants a continuation bet. Betting 200, which is roughly 36% of the pot, allows me to represent strength, potentially fold out hands like small pairs or worse, and take down the pot or gather information on the turn.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a nine kicker on a board of Q-T-2. The pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, has made a continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot, which is a standard sizing. Folding middle pair to a single bet here would be too weak, as my hand has significant showdown value and can beat many of the opponent's bluffs or semi-bluffs. Raising is not ideal because it would likely only be called by better hands (like a Queen or an overpair) and force out hands I'm currently beating. Calling is the most balanced play to see how the turn develops while keeping the pot at a manageable size.

The turn card is a Deuce of clubs, which pairs the board. This is generally a neutral card. I still have middle pair (Tens) and am out of position. Checking is the most appropriate action to continue controlling the size of the pot and to see what the aggressor, Flashlight, decides to do. Leading into the pre-flop raiser here doesn't provide much information and would likely only be called by stronger hands or result in a fold from hands I already beat.

I have Ace-high, which is essentially a bluff-catcher at this point. The board is paired, and Flash's check on the turn could indicate they are giving up on a draw or playing cautiously with a pair. Betting here as a bluff is unlikely to fold out any better hand, such as a Queen or a Ten. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and see the river for free, preserving my stack early in the tournament.

The river card is a King, which is quite unfavorable for my pair of Tens as it's now the third pair on a board of Q-T-2-2-K. On the turn, the action went check-check, suggesting Flashlight likely has a marginal made hand or a draw. By checking the river, I am looking to reach showdown as cheaply as possible. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I would be forced to fold to most raises. Checking allows Flashlight to check back with similar strength hands or potentially bluff with missed draws, which I can then evaluate.

I have Ace-high on a board of Q-T-2-2-K. The opponent has checked to me on the river. My hand is a bluff-catcher at best, as the opponent likely called the flop with a piece of this board (a pair of queens, tens, or deuces). Betting is not advisable because a pair will likely call, and I cannot expect a fold from a better hand. Checking behind is the standard play to take my Ace-high to showdown, minimizing risk while acknowledging that my hand will rarely win against a calling range, but betting would only serve to bloat the pot when I am almost certainly behind.

I have Ace-King offsuit (AKo) on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a premium starting hand and easily strong enough to raise for value, take control of the hand, and build the pot. I will make a standard 2.5x raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a 2.5x raise. In a three-handed game, ranges are wider, but T8o is still a marginal hand to play out of position against an aggressive button. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am likely to be dominated or forced to fold to continuation bets. Given that it is early in the tournament and I want to avoid unnecessary chip loss in sub-optimal spots, folding is the most disciplined play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s7c, facing a raise to 250 from the button. The pot size is 400, and I need to call 150 to see the flop. With pot odds of approximately 2.6:1, calling is a mathematically sound decision to defend my big blind against a wide button opening range. I will call and evaluate the board post-flop.

I hold bottom pair with a weak kicker on a fairly coordinated board containing a flush draw. Since I am the pre-flop caller and the aggressor has the initiative, checking is the standard play to let the original raiser act. I will evaluate my opponent's potential continuation bet before committing any more chips to the pot.

The flop of 5s Ts 6c is somewhat coordinated and hits the Big Blind defender's range relatively well, considering potential straight draws, flush draws, and mid-pairs. While I have two overcards and the backdoor nut flush draw with the Ace of Spades, my actual hand is just Ace-high. A continuation bet could fold out some better hands (like small pairs) or worse high cards, but getting check-raised would force me to fold a hand with decent equity. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and evaluate the turn card in position.

With middle pair on a board that has now brought straight possibilities, checking is the most balanced move. The opponent showed weakness by checking the flop. Betting here risks bloating a pot against a range that could easily contain a ten, a nine, or a draw that caught up, while checking allows me to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown against potential bluffs or missed draws.

Flashlight has checked twice, indicating weakness or a marginal hand. While I could attempt a delayed continuation bet to steal the pot, the board is highly coordinated (5-6-9-10) and my bet might only be called by pairs or strong draws. My Ace-King high still has some showdown value against missed broadway cards, and I have 6 clean outs to hit a top pair. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and see the river for free.

I am in the big blind with a pair of sixes on a 5s-Ts-6c-9h-Kh board. Throughout the hand, both the opponent and I have checked, indicating that neither of us has connected strongly with this board. My pair of sixes is a very weak bluff-catcher. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat (any King, Ten, Nine, or even a higher pair or Ace-high that improved). Since I cannot represent a strong hand and my hand lacks significant showdown value against anything other than a complete air-ball bluff, checking is the optimal play to reach a cheap showdown.

The King on the river gives me top pair, top kicker. Flashlight has checked all three streets, strongly indicating weakness, a missed draw, or a marginal one-pair hand like a Ten or Nine. Since I checked both the flop and turn, my river bet will look like a delayed bluff or an attempt to steal, making it highly likely that a weaker pair will call me as a bluff-catcher. I will make a value bet of around half the pot to extract value from these weaker hands.

I am holding bottom pair on a 5s-Ts-6c-9h-Kh board, which is a weak bluff-catcher. My opponent checked the flop and turn, then suddenly led out with a bet on the river after the King paired. This bet is polarized: it is either a value bet with a King, Ten, or Nine, or it is an attempt to take down the pot with a missed draw or air. Given the board texture and my opponent's range (a button open), my pair of sixes loses to almost all semi-bluffs and definitely all value hands. It is mathematically disciplined to fold here rather than calling with such a weak hand against a river bet.

I am on the button with 73o, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. In a three-handed game, while I should be opening a wide range from the button, 73o is significantly below the threshold for a profitable raise. Folding preserves my stack for more advantageous situations.

As the Small Blind, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind after the Button folded. While 5-9 offsuit is a relatively weak hand, I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call (50 to win a pot of 150). Completing the small blind is a standard strategic play in this scenario to realize equity in a heads-up pot.
With a very weak hand like T3o in the Big Blind after the Small Blind limps, there is no reason to raise and bloat the pot. Checking allows me to see a free flop and evaluate my options from there.

I hold a pair of nines on a Queen-high board with straight draw possibilities. Since the pot was limped pre-flop and the board is somewhat connected, betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like a Queen or an Eight) or hands that have significant equity against me. Checking allows me to control the pot size and realize the showdown value of my pair while minimizing risk against a potential check-raise.

The board is Qs-8h-9c, and I have T3o, giving me a gutshot straight draw if a Jack hits, but no pair or other meaningful equity. The opponent limped pre-flop and checked the flop. This board hits a Small Blind limping range quite well, as it contains many straight draws, 9x, 8x, and weak Qx hands. Betting here as a bluff is risky because of how connected the board is. Taking a free card to try and hit my gutshot or evaluate the turn is the most optimal, low-risk play.

I have improved my hand to three of a kind on the turn. With the board showing Qs-8h-9c-9h, I have a very strong holding. The pot was checked through on the flop, suggesting my opponent likely does not have a monster hand, but they might have a Queen or an Eight. Since the board is somewhat coordinated, I should start building the pot for value to get paid by any secondary pairs or draws. A bet of 120 into the 200 pot is a solid sizing to extract value while keeping the opponent's range wide.

With Ts 3c on a Qs 8h 9c 9h board, I have no pair and a very weak gutshot straight draw to an imperfect straight that might be dominated. Facing a bet of 120 into a 200 pot from the small blind who limped pre-flop and now leads on a paired turn, my hand has virtually zero equity. Folding is the only logical choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Seven suited, which is a playable hand in a 3-handed game. Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or gain position advantage. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size (3x the big blind) to put pressure on the small and big blinds.

I am dealt Queen-Four offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, it is an easy fold, as playing this out of position against a pre-flop raiser would be a losing play.

I am in the Big Blind with 3c 4d. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. Although the pot odds are roughly 2:1, 3-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands and plays very poorly out of position. Given the high stack depth and the early stage of the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.
Offsuit 9-6 is a very weak starting hand, even on the button in 3-handed play. I should fold and save my chips for better hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey of spades. Since the Button folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind. While T3s is a marginal hand, it has suited value and the cost to see a flop is only 50 chips. I will complete the bet to see a flop and keep the pot small, as raising with such a weak kicker out of position is risky.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Ten offsuit, which is a decent holding in a blind-versus-blind situation. The small blind has completed the bet, making the pot 200. Since no one has raised, checking is the standard play to see a flop for free while maintaining pot control out of position.

I have Ten-high with limited potential, only holding a backdoor spade draw and some very weak straight possibilities. The board is somewhat coordinated (7-9-Q) and likely connects with a standard Big Blind checking range. Since I am out of position and have no showdown value, checking is the most conservative and appropriate play to see if I can catch a free card on the turn.

I have top pair with a Queen-Ten offsuit on a relatively dry flop of 7s-9d-Qd. Since my opponent (the small blind) checked to me, they are likely indicating weakness or a willingness to check-call. I should bet to extract value from weaker pairs like an 8, 7, or middle pairs, and to charge any potential draws for seeing the next card. A bet of 120, which is over half the pot, is appropriate to build the pot.

I currently have Ten-high with a very weak backdoor flush draw on a board of 7s-9d-Qd. Flashlight, in the Big Blind, has bet 120 into a 200 pot. My hand has almost no equity against a standard range that would bet this flop, and I am out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling 120 chips with only a backdoor spade draw and no pair is a negative expected value play. Folding is the most disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 6h 7c, which is a very weak hand. While it's possible to attempt a steal from this position, 67 offsuit doesn't provide enough equity if called by either the small or big blind. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 7 of 150) and the deep stacks, I prefer to play conservatively and wait for better starting hands rather than bloating a pot with a marginal speculative hand.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. With Queen-Six offsuit, I have a playable hand in this heads-up scenario from the small blind. Completing the small blind is a standard approach to see a flop with a decent range, rather than folding or over-committing with a raise.

T9o is a marginal hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, especially since I will have position on the Small Blind who just limped. Raising could fold out weak hands but isolates me against better starting hands if called. Checking to see a free flop is the optimal and lowest-variance choice.

The flop is heavily coordinated with three hearts, and my Queen-high hand lacks a heart flush draw or any other significant equity. Since I am out of position, checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and avoid committing chips to a likely losing hand if the opponent shows strength. If the opponent checks behind, I get a free card, and if they bet, I can comfortably fold.

I hold the Ten of Hearts, giving me a strong but non-nut flush draw on a monotone 5h-7h-Kh board. Since the Small Blind limped pre-flop and checked the flop, their range is likely capped and heavily weighted towards missed overcards or weak pairs. Betting half the pot serves as an effective semi-bluff to take down the pot immediately, while still retaining significant equity to hit a flush if called.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three hearts and a King, making it highly likely that Pro has connected with the board, either with a King, a pair of fives or sevens, or a heart flush draw. My Queen-high hand has no equity (no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw) and I am out of position. Calling here would be a negative expected value decision. Folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

I am on the button with a pocket pair, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I want to build a pot in position and take control of the hand, so I will raise to 300 to either win the blinds immediately or play post-flop with the initiative.

I am dealt T4o in the small blind, which is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button, calling or 3-betting here would be a significantly losing play, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. The clear decision is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Flashlight opened to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, Jack-Six is a very weak hand to defend out of position against a 3x open. It has poor playability on most textures and is frequently dominated. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack, I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Q7o is a weak offsuit hand, falling well outside of standard opening ranges even from the button in 3-handed play. The best action is to fold and conserve our stack for better opportunities.

In a 3-handed game, when the button folds, the small blind has a wide opening range. K5o is a relatively strong hand in a heads-up situation against the big blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to attempt to win the pot immediately or play the hand with the initiative if called.

My hand (2d Tc) is extremely weak and poorly suited for defending against a raise out of position. Given the potential range of hands the small blind could be raising with, I am likely behind and will have significant difficulty navigating post-flop play. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal holding, so folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o) in a 3-handed game. This is a standard hand to open-raise with to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I have been using a sizing of 300 in previous hands to signal strength, and I will maintain that consistency here.

Pocket sixes is a strong enough hand to defend against a button open. Since I am out of position, calling keeps the pot manageable while offering good implied odds if I hit a set on the flop. A 3-bet would commit more chips out of position, which could be risky, so calling is the most balanced approach.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button and a flat call from the Small Blind, K7s is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position in a multi-way pot. Even though the pot odds are decent, the risk of reverse implied odds against stronger King-x hands or being dominated is too high. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve the stack for a better spot.

The flop [Ad, Ah, Tc] is highly unfavorable for my pocket sixes. Since I am out of position against an opponent who raised pre-flop, this board heavily favors their range, which likely includes many Ax combinations. My pocket pair has no showdown value and virtually no chance to improve against better hands. Checking is the most standard approach to re-evaluate after seeing the opponent's action, but given the texture, I am prepared to fold to any significant aggression.

I have middle pair (Tens and Aces) on a board that heavily favors my pre-flop opening range. Since I raised from the button, my range contains many Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) that Flashlight's flat-call from the small blind often lacks (as they might have 3-bet with strong Aces). By betting 300 into a 700 pot, I continue representing strength, extract value from straight draws like KQ or KJ, and smaller pocket pairs, while also protecting my hand. If I face a check-raise, I can re-evaluate and likely fold, as it would indicate a trips or better.

The flop texture [Ad, Ah, Tc] is extremely unfavorable for my pocket sixes. Any Ace in the opponent's range has me completely crushed, and even middle pairs or ten-high combinations are currently beating me. Given that the opponent bet into me on this board, they are representing significant strength or a strong Ace. There is virtually no equity for my pocket pair to improve to a winning hand against a calling or betting range. Calling here would be a mistake; folding is the only rational decision.

Ten-six offsuit is a very weak hand with poor potential for making strong pairs or straights. As I am on the button, I have position, but it is not worth calling a 100-chip bet to play such a dominated hand in a three-way pot. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With A2 offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a decent starting hand to attempt a steal against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flash to fold marginal holdings. If called, I still hold an Ace-high hand and can evaluate my continuation bet strategy on the flop based on the board texture.

I am in the Big Blind holding T3o. The action folded to the Small Blind (Pro), who raised to 300 (a 3x raise). While I have position on the Small Blind post-flop in this 3-handed game, T3o is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against a standard SB opening range. I need approximately 33% equity to call, but T3o is likely dominated or far behind. Since it's early in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, there's no reason to defend with such a marginal hand.

Q4 suited is a weak hand even 3-handed on the button. It has severe reverse implied odds when hitting a Queen and lacks straight potential. Standard opening ranges on the button would include Q8s+ and maybe Q7s, making Q4s a clear fold to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine of spades, a strong suited hand for a blind-vs-blind situation. Since the button folded, I will put pressure on the Big Blind by raising to 3x. This allows me to take the pot immediately or play a strong hand with initiative if called.

Holding 5h 7s is a weak, uncoordinated hand. Calling a 3x raise out of position against an aggressive opponent is negative expected value. It is best to fold, preserve my stack, and wait for a more favorable hand to engage with.

I am on the button with Jack of Diamonds and Trey of Hearts (J3o). This is a very weak starting hand, well outside of a standard button opening range. Given the tournament context and the need to play efficiently, I will fold this hand and look for better opportunities in later hands.

With the button having folded, I am now heads-up against the Big Blind. Holding suited connectors (7s, 8s), I have a hand that plays well in position or can take down the pot pre-flop if the Big Blind is weak. Raising to 350 puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to dictate the flow of the hand, maintaining my aggressive image.

Facing a 3.5x open raise from the small blind, 95 suited is too weak to defend, even with position. The gap is too wide and the cards are too low to realize enough equity against their opening range. Folding is the standard and safest play.

Ten-Trey offsuit is a very weak starting hand with little to no equity or playability. Regardless of the position or the action, folding is the clear and standard decision to preserve my stack for a more profitable spot.

With QJo in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong hand for a blind-vs-blind situation. Instead of just completing the blind, raising to 300 applies pressure to the Big Blind and allows me to take the initiative into the flop. QJo plays well heads-up and dominates many of the hands the BB might defend with.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a 3x open. While 3-handed games involve wider ranges, T7o is a marginal defending hand. Calling 200 more into a pot of 400 gives me 2:1 odds, requiring roughly 33% equity. Although T7o likely has that raw equity against a wide SB stealing range, the disadvantage of playing out of position post-flop with a weak hand that doesn't flop well makes this a fold. I prefer to wait for better spots to defend or 3-bet.
I am on the Button with 5c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Pre-flop in a 3-handed game, it's best to simply fold this trash rather than attempting a steal, especially with full stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s3c, which is a very weak hand. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to contend with. While it is only 50 chips more to call, 83 offsuit has extremely poor playability and equity. In previous hands, Flashlight has shown willingness to defend or bet when checked to. Folding is the most disciplined play here to avoid wasting chips out of position with a junk hand.

In a 3-handed game, 98o is a standard opening hand from the button. I have positional advantage post-flop and the hand has some straight potential. A 3x open is a standard size to attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am in the small blind with King-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand to defend against a standard button raise of 300. Being out of position for the remainder of the hand makes playing this marginal holding difficult and potentially expensive. Given the current stack depths and my tournament strategy, the best decision is to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.
54o is a very weak starting hand to play out of position against a 3x raise. The best decision is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Jack-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand with extremely low playability. Even in a blind-steal position like the button, committing chips to the pot with this hand is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

8d 6c offsuit is a very weak hand. Being in the Small Blind, I would have to play out of position against the Big Blind if I call or raise. Given the low equity and poor playability of this hand, folding is the optimal decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

2c 3c is a very weak hand even on the button. While it's suited, it doesn't offer enough playability or equity to justify an open raise. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 4s and the Button has folded. This is a standard 'blind vs. blind' situation. My hand is strong enough to attempt a steal against the Big Blind (Flashlight), who has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises in previous hands (Hands #9, #12, #16). Raising to 300 has been my consistent sizing and is likely to win the pot immediately or put me in a strong position post-flop.

Holding 2s-Tc is a very weak hand with poor playability. While I am getting 2:1 on a call, the risk of playing a dominated hand out of position against an aggressive opponent outweighs the pot odds. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision here.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack of diamonds, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. I have been consistently opening for 3x the big blind in previous hands. I will continue this strategy to build a pot with my strong hand and exert pressure on the blinds.

5h 9d is a weak hand and playing it out of position in the small blind against a button open is not a profitable long-term strategy. Flash has been opening frequently, but I need to be disciplined to preserve my stack for better opportunities. Folding is the most standard and profitable play here.

Jack-Queen offsuit is a marginal hand when facing a 3x raise from the Button, especially since we will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. While Flash seems to be opening frequently, QJo is vulnerable to domination by stronger broadways (AJ, AQ, KQ) and has reverse implied odds. Folding here is a safe play to preserve our stack and wait for better spots.

J9 offsuit is a marginal hand that does not play well post-flop, especially in a 3-handed game against opponents who have shown aggression. With 100 chips required to call, I prefer to conserve my stack of 9,100 and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.

With pocket sixes in the small blind and the button folding, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise. A raise to 3x (300 chips) is standard to take initiative, deter the big blind from realizing equity with random overcards, and potentially win the pot immediately. If called, I can navigate post-flop cautiously while hunting for a set.

I have a pocket pair (4c, 4s) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, pocket pairs are quite strong, and I am getting 2:1 odds to call. Furthermore, in this specific dynamic (SB vs BB), I will have post-flop position over Pro for the rest of the hand. While 44 is vulnerable to many overcards, it is too strong to fold to a single raise, and calling allows me to set-mine or take the pot away on favorable textures. I will call the additional 200.

With pocket sixes on a Qd 8s 2d board, I have third pair. Being out of position against the Big Blind, this is a marginal made hand that does not warrant a continuation bet. If I bet, I will likely only get called or raised by better hands (Qx, 8x, flush draws, or higher pocket pairs) and fold out hands I am already beating. Checking allows me to exercise pot control, keep the pot small, and evaluate my opponent's action. If he checks behind, we can see a turn card cheaply; if he bets aggressively, I can comfortably fold.

I have an underpair (4c 4s) on a Qd 8s 2d board. Pro raised pre-flop from the Small Blind and has now checked to me. This check could indicate a variety of hands, including missed overcards (like AK/AJ), a medium-strength pair (like an 8), or a trap with a Queen or better. Betting here doesn't accomplish much; hands that are better than mine (Qx, 8x, 99-JJ) are unlikely to fold, and hands that are worse (missed high cards) are currently being beaten by my pocket pair. By checking back, I keep the pot small, realize my equity, and maintain control of the hand in position. I can re-evaluate on the turn based on the card and Pro's action.

The turn brings a third diamond to the board (4d), and my pocket sixes hold no diamond. My hand has very little showdown value now, and betting as a bluff without a diamond blocker is unnecessarily risky. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and evaluate my opponent's action. If he bets, I can comfortably fold.

On the turn, I have improved to a set of fours. Although the board now shows three diamonds (2d, Qd, 4d), making a flush possible, Pro's check on both the flop and the turn suggests a lack of top-tier strength. I am likely ahead of hands like Qx, 8x, or mid-pocket pairs. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to extract value from those hands and to charge any opponent holding a single diamond who might be looking to complete a flush on the river.

The turn completes the possible diamond flush, and my opponent has shown aggression with a bet of 400. Holding pocket sixes with no diamond, my hand has extremely poor equity against his betting range, which likely includes higher pairs, flushes, or strong semi-bluffs. Folding is the only optimal decision, as planned.
6h 2c is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Even from the button, there is no value in playing this hand. Folding is the strictly optimal play here to preserve my stack.

It is folded to me in the Small Blind with 8d Ts. In a three-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open-raise against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size in previous hands and maintains an aggressive table image while putting pressure on Flashlight to defend their blind.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. The pot odds are favorable (2:1), and my hand (Jack-Seven offsuit) is reasonable to defend given the range of the small blind, who has been raising frequently. Calling allows me to see the flop with position, which is a standard defensive play in this situation.

I have flopped top two pair (Tens and Eights) on a dry board (8s-Tc-5d). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot from the blinds. On this paired and monotone turn board, my pair of sixes has very little value and I have no flush draw to fall back on. Betting here would only isolate me against better hands like an 8, a flush, or a higher pair. Taking a free card by checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize the equity of my gutshot straight draw or two-pair outs.

The board has four clubs and is paired, making it extremely wet. I only have a pair of 3s with an Ace kicker. Betting here would only be called by a flush or a better hand, so there is no value. Checking is the only prudent decision to reach showdown.

The river brings a fourth club to the board. I only have two pair (eights and sixes) with no club in my hand. Any single club, a nine, or a higher pocket pair beats me. Flashlight has checked all the way down, meaning they likely don't have a strong hand, but they could easily have a weak club or are trapping with a better pair. Betting here serves no purpose, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. Checking behind is the only sensible option to try and get to a cheap showdown.

I am on the button with T6o. While it's a weak hand, this is a standard spot to apply pressure or fold. Looking at the stack sizes, Flashlight has about 55 BBs and Pro has over 150 BBs. My stack of 88 BBs is healthy. In the last several orbits, most pre-flop raises to 300 have been winning the blinds immediately. However, with a hand as weak as T6o and two active opponents behind me, including a very large stack in the Big Blind who has been aggressive, folding is the most disciplined and lowest-variance play to preserve my stack as we approach the end of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind position, and the Button (Flash) has folded. This leaves me heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). I have Ace-Four offsuit, which is a strong enough hand in this short-handed situation to attempt to take down the blinds immediately. By raising to 300, I put pressure on the Big Blind and take the initiative in the hand, aiming to build my stack.

Flashlight raises from the small blind. I hold ten-seven suited in the big blind, which is a reasonable hand to defend with, especially since I will have positional advantage post-flop. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), and my hand has good playability with both flush and straight potentials. A call is the standard and most profitable decision here.

The flop of 7h-3c-Kc is relatively dry and favors my pre-flop opening range, as I would frequently have a King in my range. As the pre-flop aggressor, I am continuation betting to maintain initiative and represent strength, putting pressure on Pro's calling range. Ace-high is currently the best hand against many of Pro's potential holdings, and if Pro raises, I can easily fold, keeping my losses minimized.

I flopped middle pair along with a ten-high flush draw, giving me significant equity. Flashlight fired a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. While a raise could be viable as a semi-bluff, calling is highly profitable here. It keeps their bluffs in the pot, controls the pot size, and allows me to utilize my positional advantage on the turn.

The turn card (5c) has completed a potential flush draw and brought more connectivity to the board. My hand (Ac-4h) now has a flush draw (due to the Ac) and a gutshot straight draw. Checking is the most prudent play here as it allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity with a free card if the opponent also checks, and avoid bloating the pot against a range that likely includes Kings or better. Betting into a board that just became wetter with the 5c is risky and likely to be called by any pair, which is currently beating me.

I turned a ten-high flush on a board with three clubs (Kc, 5c, 3c). Flashlight checked to me, signaling weakness or pot control. I have a very strong hand, but I am vulnerable to higher single-club draws like the Ac, Qc, or Jc. I need to bet to build the pot for value against hands like Kx, two pair, or naked club draws, while charging them to see the river. Betting half the pot (600) is a solid sizing to accomplish these goals.

I am facing a 600 bet into a 1200 pot, meaning I am getting 3:1 pot odds. My hand contains the Ace of clubs, giving me the nut flush draw on this club-heavy board, as well as a gutshot straight draw. Combined, I have approximately 12 outs, which provides me with roughly 27% equity, satisfying the pot odds requirement of 25% to make a profitable call. Given the mathematical strength of my draw, I will call to realize my equity and potentially take the pot on the river.

The board is 7h-3c-Kc-5c-8d. I hold Ace-high (Ac-4h), which is very weak on this texture. Since I checked to the opponent on the river and the opponent has not bet, I will check again to take it to showdown. Betting here as a bluff is unlikely to be effective against a range that called a bet on the flop and bet on the turn, as the opponent likely has at least a King or a pair. Checking is the only viable option to minimize potential losses.

I have a ten-high flush on a board with only three clubs, meaning my opponent needs exactly two higher clubs in their hand to beat me. The board didn't pair on the river, eliminating full house possibilities. My opponent's turn call suggests they might have a strong made hand like AK, a set, or two pair, possibly holding a single high club that missed the river. I have a clear value bet here. Sizing to half the pot (1200) targets these weaker made hands that can still find a call, while safely folding to a shove if they happen to hold a higher flush.

The board texture is 7h-3c-Kc-5c-8d, and my Ace-high is extremely weak at showdown. The opponent has shown consistent aggression by calling the flop, betting the turn, and firing a large bet on the river, suggesting a made hand like a King or better. With no draws completed and no reason to believe I have the best hand, calling a significant bet on the river is a negative expected value move. I will fold to preserve my remaining stack.

I am on the button with A7o, which is a playable hand in late position. Given that my stack of 4400 is the shortest at the table, I need to be proactive to increase my stack size. Opening with a raise is superior to limping, as it allows me to potentially take down the blinds or isolate an opponent while maintaining the betting initiative. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size.
I am in the Small Blind with Td 2d, which is a very weak hand. The Button raised to 3 big blinds. Out of position with a trash hand, the only sensible move is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with a premium hand, Ace-King offsuit. Flashlight has raised from the button, which is a common steal position. Given the strength of my hand and the stack sizes, a 3-bet is the standard play for value and protection. I will raise to 1000 to put pressure on Flashlight's 4100-chip stack and take control of the pot.

I opened with A7o on the button, and Flash responded with a 3-bet from the big blind. My hand, while playable in a vacuum, is quite marginal against a 3-betting range that likely contains stronger Aces or pocket pairs. Given that I am the short stack with 4100 chips, committing a large portion of my stack to a 3-bet pot with A7o is high-variance and often negative expected value. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 3800 chips and wait for a more favorable spot to double up or build my stack.

With pocket fours on the Button and a deep stack, an open-raise is standard to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll make it 3x the big blind, consistent with previous open sizes.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five of hearts. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. While Q5 suited has some potential in a 3-handed game, calling here puts me out of position against the chip leader with a marginal hand. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending with a weak Queen-high hand.

I am in the big blind with K2o, which is a weak hand. With a raise to 300 from the Button and no other action, I am getting decent pot odds (200 to win 450), but playing K2o out of position against an aggressive opponent with a much larger stack is unfavorable. Folding preserves my remaining 4000 stack for a better spot.

In 3-handed play, the button should open with a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds. However, 97o is significantly below the standard opening threshold for this position. Given that Flashlight has a shorter stack and might look to shove over an open, and Pro is the chip leader who has shown a willingness to defend or 3-bet, folding this weak offsuit hand is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities in the final stages of the tournament.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. K3 suited is a perfectly playable hand in this position. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take down the blinds and apply pressure, rather than just completing the small blind and allowing the big blind a free play.

J2s is a very weak hand with poor playability, even when defending the big blind against a small blind open. Flashlight's 3x raise signals either a solid holding or a standard steal, but J2s is easily dominated and not worth the investment. Folding is the most profitable decision here.

I am in the button position with a short stack of 4100 chips. Holding Jack-high offsuit (Js, 8d) is a weak hand to open with in a three-handed game, especially when I need to preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity. Folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound move given the strength of the hand.

With 76o in the Small Blind after the Button folds, my hand is too weak to justify a steal against the Big Blind. The best and standard play to preserve my stack and maximize EV is simply to fold and wait for better spots.
I am on the Button with an extremely weak offsuit hand (3-6 offsuit). With two players left to act behind me, the best play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While the Button has folded and I only need to put in 50 more chips to see a flop, this hand has almost no post-flop playability and very little equity against even a wide Big Blind defending range. With only 18 hands remaining in the tournament and a comfortable stack, there is no reason to play such a weak hand out of position.
I'm on the button with Ace-Five offsuit. In three-handed play, this is a standard opening hand. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially pick up the blinds.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit, which is a weak hand to defend with out of position against a button raise. The big blind has yet to act, creating a risk of being squeezed or trapped. Given my current stack and the position disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, I have no equity to defend out of position. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I should look to steal the blinds and take initiative pre-flop. A standard raise size of 300 is appropriate to apply pressure and potentially pick up the blinds, or play a pot in position if called.

With 98 offsuit in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button, my hand is too weak and disconnected to call profitably out of position. The button is also playing a relatively short stack, making a 3-bet bluff unappealing since they might just shove. Folding is the standard and safest option here.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4h2c. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) has folded. Flashlight is a relatively short stack with 3800 chips. My hand is very weak and does not play well post-flop, especially against a button range that has shown aggression. There is no reason to defend this hand and risk chips given the proximity to the end of the tournament and the weakness of my holding.

With Q8o on the button, this hand is quite marginal for a steal and plays poorly post-flop if called or 3-bet. While I have a large chip lead, there's no need to force the action with weak offsuit holdings. Folding is the disciplined play.

I have QsTc in the small blind. Pro has already folded on the button. I am facing the small blind completion (50 chips) against Flashlight in the big blind. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack (4150) compared to mine (9100) and the Pro's stack (16600), QTo is a strong hand to play heads-up. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the big blind, which has been a standard opening size in this game.

Holding 8-3 offsuit in the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind is a very weak hand with no significant equity. Given the tournament stage and my relatively short stack, I need to be selective with my hands. Folding here is the most disciplined play to conserve my remaining chips for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit (KTs). This is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack of 9250 chips. Standard play here is to raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Both Flashlight and Pro have been active, but a raise from the button usually yields high fold equity or allows me to play the pot in position post-flop.

I am in the small blind with a decent offsuit connector hand (T9). Given the pot size and the cost of the call (250 into a pot of 450), it is mathematically profitable to see a flop. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, the hand strength justifies the call to continue in the hand.

Squeezing with AJo from the Big Blind after a Button open and Small Blind flat is a strong play. It capitalizes on fold equity, isolates an opponent if called, and avoids playing a multi-way pot out of position with an offsuit broadway hand that can be tricky to navigate post-flop. I'll size my 3-bet to 1200 to put maximum pressure on both players, especially the relatively short-stacked SB.

I initially raised to 300 with King-Ten offsuit (Ks Td) from the button. Flashlight called from the small blind, and now Pro has three-bet to 1200 from the big blind. This is a significant raise that represents strength. Flashlight's stack is relatively short (~3850), while Pro has a massive lead. While KTo is a decent hand, it is frequently dominated by Pro's three-betting range (AQ, AJ, AK, JJ+). Calling 900 more puts a lot of chips at risk out of position or in a bloated pot against a big stack who is being aggressive. Given the tournament stage and my current stack size, I should avoid high-variance spots against the chip leader without a premium hand.

My holding of Ten-Nine offsuit is not strong enough to contest a substantial re-raise (3-bet) from the Big Blind, especially when out of position. While the pot odds of 900 to win a total pot of 2700 might seem tempting, the range of a player raising to 1200 after an initial raise and a call is likely quite strong (typically premium pairs or high cards). Calling here would be a negative expected value (-EV) play given the positional disadvantage and the strength of the opponent's range. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and optimal strategic decision.

My hand (2h, 3c) is among the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given that I am on the button and have a short stack, I need to preserve my remaining chips for better opportunities rather than contesting a pot with such low-equity cards.

With Q6o in the Small Blind, the hand is too weak to profitably play out of position against the Big Blind. Despite having a dominant chip lead, it is best to avoid bleeding chips with marginal or weak offsuit hands. Folding is the optimal decision.

With AQo on the Button, I hold a premium starting hand in 3-handed play. A standard open raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) is optimal to build the pot for value, apply pressure to the blinds, and maintain the initiative going into the flop with position.

With King-Six offsuit in the Small Blind, I am facing a button raise to 300 from Pro. Pro has been quite active and is the chip leader. My stack is healthy at 89.5 big blinds, but Flashlight in the Big Blind is short-stacked with only 37.5 big blinds. Calling here would put me out of position against a strong player, and King-Six is a relatively weak hand to defend with given the pot odds and the threat of a squeeze or a jam from the short-stacked Big Blind. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities in the final hands of the tournament.

I hold Ace-King suited, a premium hand that is ideal for a 3-bet against a button open. With 37.5 big blinds, I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips rather than just calling. A 3-bet to 1,000 puts pressure on my opponent and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or play a large pot with the initiative.

With AQo on the Button facing a 3-bet from the short-stacked Big Blind, we hold a very strong hand that performs well against a short stack's resteal range. Flashlight's 3-bet commits a large portion of their stack, meaning the SPR going to the flop will be very low. By 4-bet shoving, we fully realize our equity, fold out any of their bluffs that still have equity against us, and apply maximum pressure for their tournament life.

I have already committed 1000 chips to the pot with a premium hand (Ace-King suited). With a total stack of only 2850 and pot odds that justify the call, folding here would be an significant strategic error. My opponent's 4-bet does not outweigh the equity of my hand in a heads-up situation, making an all-in call the only viable path to maximizing my tournament life and stack potential.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at approximately 90 big blinds, while Flashlight is relatively short with about 38 big blinds. I will open for the standard 3x raise of 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds, while retaining the advantage of position for the rest of the hand.

Holding 3-2 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With an open raise from the button and being out of position in the small blind, calling is a negative expected value (-EV) play. It is much better to fold and conserve my stack for a more favorable spot given the late stage of the tournament.

I am dealt a very weak offsuit hand (6h 4c) in the Big Blind. Facing a standard 3x open raise from the Button, my hand has extremely poor equity and playability out of position. The correct strategic decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-five offsuit. While not a premium hand, it is perfectly acceptable for a button steal attempt given the small stack size and the late stage of the tournament (hand 140/150). Increasing aggression to pick up the blinds is necessary to build my stack, and a standard raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing to test the blinds.
I am in the Small Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This is an easy and automatic fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh8h, a solid suited queen. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, leaving me to call 200 more. Flashlight's stack is relatively short (3525), while I have a healthy stack of 9000. My hand has good playability post-flop, especially in a heads-up pot where I have position or at least close to it in terms of closing the action. I'm getting decent pot odds (200 to win 450, or roughly 2.25:1) and since I am defending the Big Blind, calling is the standard play with this hand to see a flop.

On a flop of 4h 5h Jc, I have a flush draw with Qh8h. The pot is 650. I am out of position against Flashlight, who raised pre-flop. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. If they bet, I have the equity to call and potentially realize my draw or bluff later. For now, checking allows me to control the pot size while holding a strong draw.

I have bottom pair with a Queen kicker and a backdoor flush draw. The board is relatively dry, but contains straight and flush draws. Since I am in position and the pot is small (650 chips), checking behind is the optimal play to maintain pot control and realize my equity. Betting with such a weak pair only serves to bloat the pot when I am likely ahead of air but behind any Ace, King, or better pair, so I will take the free card.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 4d. Flashlight checked back on the flop (4h 5h Jc), which suggests they likely do not have a strong Jack or a high pocket pair, or they are playing cautiously. I still hold a Queen-high flush draw and two overcards to the small pair on board. By checking again, I am looking to see a free river card or potentially call a small bet to realize my equity. Leading out now might force out hands I'm ahead of (like total air) but get called by hands that have me beat (like a Jack or a pocket pair). Checking maintains pot control and allows me to see how Flashlight responds to the board pairing.

I am in position on the button with a pair of 5s on a 4-4-5-J board. My opponent, Flash, has checked to me on the turn. My pair has marginal showdown value, but it is vulnerable to many overcards and straight draws. Betting here does not accomplish much, as I am unlikely to be called by worse and I risk folding out hands that are currently bluffing. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity to see the river for free.

On a river board of 4h 5h Jc 4d 4s, the board has trip fours. My best hand is 4-4-4-J-Q (using my Queen as the second kicker). While I have the second-best possible kicker to the trips on board (after an Ace or King), I am vulnerable to any pocket pair (which makes a full house) or any Ace/King high. Given that Flashlight raised pre-flop but checked both the flop and the turn, it is possible they are holding a weak Ace or a small pocket pair that they were playing cautiously. There is little value in betting here as I am unlikely to get called by worse, and I probably cannot fold out better hands like an Ace-high. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown cheaply.

The board shows three Fours (4h-4d-4s), a Five (5h), and a Jack (Jc). Holding 5c-Qc, my best hand is 4-4-4-5-5, a full house. Any Jack in my opponent's hand would result in a superior full house (4-4-4-J-J), and any Four would constitute four-of-a-kind. Since my opponent has checked, they likely lack a Jack or a Four. Betting here is risky as it only gets called by hands that beat me or results in a split pot if they also have a Five, while checking secures a showdown.
J3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Even from the button, there is no value in playing this hand when both blinds are still to act. I will fold and look for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 7c. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight) to contend with. Flashlight has a stack of about 40 big blinds and has shown a pattern of folding to pre-flop raises from the SB in previous hands (e.g., hands 139, 135, 133). While T7o is a mediocre hand, a standard raise to 300 often wins the pot immediately. If I am called, I can reassess on the flop, but the primary goal here is to exert pressure on the shorter stack and collect the blinds.
Flash raised from the small blind. I am in the big blind with Kd5d, which is a playable suited hand. Getting 2:1 odds on a call, I have sufficient equity to see a flop and defend my big blind.

On this monotone 6s 3s 9s board, I have zero spades and no connection to the board with my Td 7c. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, betting into a monotone board without a flush or a high spade is risky, especially since Flashlight has shown a willingness to defend. If I bet and get called, I'm essentially drawing dead or near-dead on most turns. Checking is the most disciplined play here to see how Flashlight reacts. If he checks back, I get a free card; if he bets, I can comfortably fold without wasting more chips on a weak hand in a bad spot.

The flop is highly coordinated with three spades, making it a very dangerous board. With Kd5d and no spades in my hand, I have almost no equity against any range that contains even a single spade. Flash checked to me, which could be a trap or a check-fold. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and avoid overcommitting to a board where I am likely trailing significantly.

The turn 4c is a blank for my hand. I have Td 7c with no spades on a board that shows 6s 3s 9s. Since the flop went check-check, it's possible Flashlight also missed or is being cautious. However, I have zero equity and no real draw. Betting here as a bluff into a board that is very likely to have hit a Big Blind's calling range (which often includes many random spades) is too risky given the current stack sizes. I will check and see if he checks back. If he bets, I am folding.

The board texture is highly coordinated with three spades, creating a dangerous environment. My King-high is currently weak, and although I possess a gutshot straight draw (needing a 2 or 7), I lack a spade to protect against any flush. Flash has checked both the flop and the turn, signaling potential weakness or a desire to control the pot. Betting here as a bluff into two potential ranges on a three-spade board is high-risk. Checking behind allows me to see the river card cheaply while maintaining pot control and minimizing further losses if I am behind.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board (8s). I have no spades in my hand, meaning I lose to any single spade held by Flashlight. Given the passive checking on the flop and turn, it's possible Flashlight also lacks a spade, but betting here into a four-flush board with Ten-high and no spade is extremely risky. I will check and hope for a check-back to see a showdown, but I will fold if Flashlight bets, as I have no equity to call.

The board shows four spades (6s 3s 9s 8s). With Kd 5d and no spade, I have zero showdown value. Flash has checked the river, signaling they likely do not have a strong flush or are checking to induce a bluff. Betting into a four-spade board with no spade as a bluff is high variance and likely ineffective against an opponent who called pre-flop. Checking behind allows me to conclude the hand without further risk, as I cannot win a showdown unless the pot is split, which is unlikely.

I am on the button with T9o, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack of 91bb. Raising to 3x (300) allows me to take control of the hand, utilize my positional advantage, and potentially take down the blinds. Both opponents have been somewhat active, but Pro (the chip leader) has shown he can be pushed off hands, and Flashlight is short enough that a raise puts significant pressure on him.

I am in the small blind with a weak, disconnected hand (3s, Qh). Calling the 250 raise from the button OOP would be negative expected value as I will rarely hit a strong enough flop to continue comfortably. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve my stack.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Button open. This is a premium hand 3-handed. I want to 3-bet for value, to take control of the hand, and to deny equity to weaker hands. Out of position, a 4x 3-bet to 1200 is standard and puts significant pressure on the Button's opening range.

I opened to 300 from the button with T9o, which is a standard play in a three-handed game. However, Pro in the big blind has responded with a 4x 3-bet to 1200. While I have the positional advantage, T9o is a marginal hand that performs poorly against a polarized or strong 3-betting range from the chip leader. Given that Pro has shown significant aggression with this sizing in previous hands (e.g., Hand #82, #136) and my stack is healthy but not the largest, there is no need to defend with a hand that is easily dominated. I will fold and wait for a better spot to challenge the leader's aggression.

With a short stack and a weak, uncoordinated hand like 5s-Td in the button position, there is no strategic value in calling the 100 chips to see a flop. The best play is to conserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

I have pocket Aces, the best possible starting hand. With the button folding, I am first to act from the small blind. I want to build the pot and extract maximum value from the big blind, so a standard raise to 3 big blinds is the optimal play.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I only need to call 200 more, J3o does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack of 8700, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong starting hand. Raising to 300 is a standard open for me and puts pressure on the blinds. If they 3-bet, I can evaluate further based on their stack sizes.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens (Qh, Qc), which is a premium starting hand. Pro, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. Given my position in the Small Blind and the strength of my hand, a 3-bet is the most effective play to build the pot and extract value. I want to price out speculative hands and potentially isolate Pro or induce a shove from the short-stacked Flashlight in the Big Blind. I will raise to 1000.

Flash has 3-bet the small blind after Pro's open, and I am in the big blind with AQo. With only 3725 chips left at hand 144 of 150, I am essentially pot-committed. A 4-bet shove is the most strategic play to maximize fold equity and ensure I realize the equity of my premium hand against a range that may be wider than usual given the late stage of the tournament.

I opened with ATo on the button, but now I'm facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind and a 4-bet from the Big Blind. ATo is simply too weak to continue against this much aggression, especially with the Small Blind still left to act behind me who could potentially shove their remaining stack. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I hold Pocket Queens, which is the third-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I initially 3-bet to 1000 to extract value and narrow the field. Flashlight has responded with a 4-bet to 3725, which represents almost their entire stack. Pro has folded, leaving it heads-up. Against a short stack shove in this late stage of the tournament (Hand 144/150), QQ is far too strong to fold. I am likely ahead of Flashlight's range, which includes smaller pairs and hands like Ace-King or Ace-Queen. I will call the raise and look to win a significant pot.

I have a dominant overpair with Pocket Queens on a safe, low-card board (7h, 3s, 6h). During the pre-flop action, I called Flashlight's 4-bet, and they are now left with only 100 chips. Given the massive pot of 7750, I am mathematically committed to this hand and will bet 100 to put Flashlight all-in and proceed to the showdown.

With only 100 chips remaining in my stack against a pot of 7850, the pot odds are approximately 78:1, making this an mathematically mandatory call regardless of my hand's strength. Holding Ace-high with two overcards, I have sufficient equity to justify calling the small bet, as folding would be a massive strategic error in such a lopsided pot odds situation.

In a heads-up situation with only two players remaining, King-high is a strong hand from the button. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size throughout the tournament, and it allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.
